I use to watch series and films in English to learn. In my actual point for me is very easy to watch a movie with English subtitles but hard to watch it without subtitles. I got and idea to make an intermediate point between those two methods.

We can make a simple software to spoil the .srt (subtitles file), the software change random words to a lines rows.

For example:

1 00:02:12,087 --> 00:02:13,964 - Jump! - What are you doing?

2 00:02:24,727 --> 00:02:26,718 - Oh, my God! - What are you doing?

3 00:02:28,527 --> 00:02:29,721 Help me!

2 00:02:24,727 --> 00:02:26,718 - Oh, __ God! - What ___ you _____?

What do you think? It seems easy with your library.

issue_title: 2D Wavelet Filtering
issue_body:
Hi again!
I've been trying to apply the Wavelet filtering to my original 2D MRI cardiac image. I have been running the following code:

```
for decompositionImage, decompositionName, inputKwargs in imageoperations.getWaveletImage(cropped_image, cropped_mask):
    waveletFirstOrderFeaturs = firstorder.RadiomicsFirstOrder(decompositionImage, cropped_mask, **inputKwargs)
    waveletFirstOrderFeaturs.enableAllFeatures()
    waveletFirstOrderFeaturs.calculateFeatures()

    print('Calculated firstorder features with wavelet: ', decompositionName)
    for (key, val) in six.iteritems(waveletFirstOrderFeaturs.featureValues):
        waveletFeatureName = '%s_%s' % (str(decompositionName), key)
        print('  ', waveletFeatureName, ':', val)
```

and the shape features are calculated correctly. My question regards more the interpretation of these. As a matter of fact, 9 different shape_feature_groups are computed (LLL, LLH, LHL, LHH, HLL, HLH, HHL, HHH, LLL). My questions are:

1) How do I interpret the third letter of the filter (e.g. the H in LLH) since I only have a 2D image? Shouldn't I have just 4 filtered images (LL, LH, HL, HH)?

2) Why the LLL is computed twice?

3) Is there a reference article where the H and L filtering is well explained? From what I understood, the function applies a cascade filtering bank (e.g. HL means that first a high-pass filter and then a low-pass filter are applied to the image), but I'm not sure.

Thanks a lot in advance!

issue_title: New error when mixing gl and gles on web service
issue_body:
I am working with an open source project called [lovr](https://github.com/bjornbytes/lovr) which [uses glad](https://github.com/bjornbytes/lovr/tree/master/src/lib/glad). Its current GLAD generation link is [this URL](http://glad.dav1d.de/#profile=core&language=c&specification=gl&api=gl%3D3.3&api=gles2%3D3.0&extensions=GL_ARB_texture_storage&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_compression_s3tc&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_filter_anisotropic&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_sRGB&extensions=GL_OVR_multiview
).

Sometime in the last week or three, this URL stopped working. Attempting to generate a GLAD from this URL on the web service now results in a message

> Warnings/Errors:
> Core profile not available when selecting any gles API, automatically changed

It then generates a Compatibility profile.

When I spoke to the lovr developer, he explained that the goal was to get a Core profile when running with plain GL and whatever profile when running with gles2. This is because the code targets both desktops and on emscripten, and Desktop-OpenGL Core is more similar to gles2 than OpenGL Compatibility is.

There are two issues here:
- What would be the correct way for lovr to get its gl core + gles2-supporting headers? I don't know what it was the web service was doing before when it simply allowed "gles2 core", but it *worked*.
- *Even if what Lovr is doing is incorrect*, it is problematic for there to be URLs which previously worked on the web service but now no longer work. I maintain a fork of Lovr where I use additional extensions that Lovr master does not use. Previously I could add or remove extensions by loading Lovr's URL up and changing the extensions list to add or remove one or two extensions. However now running the URL produces a compatibility profile, which is not what Lovr targets. In other words, removing support for core+gles outputs means third party consumers of older GLAD-using projects are "stuck"— I cannot necessarily patch Lovr to migrate it away from "core+gles2" because I do not directly control the original project, but I can't generate a new core+gles2 source file either.

I am not certain what my "expected behavior" here is, but: Would it be possible to alter the web service so that generating the "illegal" core+gles2 profile either becomes a *warning* (ie emits the illegal profile rather than silently altering it to compatibility) or provides more useful advice about what to do?

Thanks

issue_title: SExtractor uses minimum sig when computing windowed positions
issue_body:
There are some discrepancies in windowed position parameters for objects with small Kron radii. This was originally discussed in #37.

It looks like SExtractor effectively sets a minimum for the `sig` parameter. In the original codebase, in `compute_winpos`, `sig` is set to

```c
sig = obj2->hl_radius*2.0/2.35; /* From half-FWHM to sigma */
```

when computing the half-light radius, there are checks on the minimum and maximum value

```c
    if (obj2->hl_radius < GROWTH_MINHLRAD)
      obj2->hl_radius = GROWTH_MINHLRAD;
    }
```

`GROWTH_MINHLRAD` is 0.5 pixels, so the minimum `sig` value is 0.5*2.0/2.35. We should try setting this in the tests to see if it resolves the discrepancy.

Assuming it does, we can either simply document the SExtractor behavior, or hard-code this minimum in `sep.winpos` and add documentation about it.

issue_title: Unneeded conditional statement calling parse_args
issue_body:
In config.py:578:

    if fake_args:
      self.cliargs = cliparser.parse_args(fake_args)
    else:
      self.cliargs = cliparser.parse_args()

where fake_args is None by default, however from the documentation of the arguments parser:

https://docs.python.org/2/library/argparse.html#argparse.ArgumentParser.parse_args

    ArgumentParser.parse_args(args=None, namespace=None)

So calling directly to the following (without conditions):

    self.cliargs = cliparser.parse_args(fake_args)

Should be the same.

---

Also the comparisons with 'None' should be carried out with the 'is' statement:

    if fake_args is not None:

I hope it can helps!

issue_title: Widget not displaying with latest jupyter notebook
issue_body:
The widget doesn't appear at all with the latest jupyter notebook. Not sure exactly where in the stack of things something is going wrong, but it can be reproduced with:

```python
import MDAnalysis as mda
import nglview as nv
from nglview.datafiles import PDB, XTC

u = mda.Universe(PDB, XTC)

protein = u.select_atoms('protein')

w = nv.show_mdanalysis(protein)

issue_title: add show_file
Note about NGLModel
Add a compatibility shim for `commands.getstatusoutput`?
issue_body:
`six` currently includes a not-yet-released change to add `six.moves` entries for `commands.getoutput` and `commands.getstatusoutput`: https://github.com/benjaminp/six/commit/e05c7253043647e37bdbc13038bff06334fac8cb

While this is correct for `commands.getoutput`,  `subprocess.getstatusoutput` unfortunately broke backwards compatibility with Python 2's `commands.getstatusoutput` back in 3.3.4 (see https://bugs.python.org/issue22635 for details), which means the Python 3 API needs a compatibility shim to shift the exit code left by 8 bits to get it back into the format produced by `os.wait()`: https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.wait

Accordingly, a backwards compatible `getstatusoutput` implementation on Py3 actually looks like this:

    def py2_getstatusoutput(cmd):
        status, output = subprocess.getstatusoutput(command)
        if os.name != 'nt':
            # convert status to be interpreted according to the wait() rules
            status = status << 8
        return status, output

I'm not sure how to incorporate that into the `six.moves` structure, though :(

issue_title: IncrementalEndpoint start_time arg for timezone aware datetime
issue_body:
When providing a timezone aware datetime for `start_time` to `IncrementalEndpoint`, an incorrect value is sent to Zendesk when the systems local timezone does not match the datetime's timezone. This is highlighted in http://stackoverflow.com/a/11743262 and J.F. Sebastian's comment there.

Line affected: https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/1.1.7/zenpy/lib/endpoint.py#L120

Demo of problem
```python
import pytz
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# create a shared reference point
start_time = datetime.now()

# by assigning timezone, its actually provides a different meaning to the time
utc_start_time = start_time.replace(tzinfo=pytz.timezone('UTC'))
eastern_start_time = start_time.replace(tzinfo=pytz.timezone('US/Eastern'))
assert utc_start_time != eastern_start_time  # passes
assert (utc_start_time - eastern_start_time) != timedelta(0)

# NOTE: my system timezone is US/Eastern, so the epoch_eastern_start_time is actually the correct value
epoch_utc_start_time = utc_start_time.strftime("%s")
epoch_eastern_start_time = eastern_start_time.strftime("%s")
assert epoch_utc_start_time != epoch_eastern_start_time  # fails
```

Workaround: Calculate `start_time` yourself correctly such that a datetime object isn't provided

issue_title: Do not write fusesoc.log by default
issue_body:
Fusesoc by default writes a file 'fusesoc.log' into the working directory. Even though this is common for many EDA tools, I suggest removing this functionality by default.

Why?
- Having two fusesoc runs in the same directory results in an "interleaved" fusesoc.log file, essentially a useless one.
- It's very uncommon behavior for any "non-EDA" tool.

Two ideas how to resolve this:
- Write the fusesoc.log file to the build directory (`build/DESIGNNAME/...`)
- Don't write a fusesoc.log file unless instructed so by e.g. a command line option (e.g. `--logfile=fusesoc.log`)

issue_title: Permanent Content-type duplication in response after 404
issue_body:
**Description**
In the case of a general 404 trying to access a file that doesn't exist, Cling incorrectly adds an additional `Content-type: text/plain` after every 404 received. This means that if the server sends 3 404 responses, the header will provide 3 `Content-type: text/plain` headers. For 10,000 responses, 10,000 duplicate headers. 

This is persistent until the Headers variable is cleared or the service is restarted. 

**Fix**
I believe that in the LOC below, `self.message` is never False so in every case, it will append another value. 

https://github.com/rmohr/static3/blob/309ddc3e14384f5fecf99afddb2c17ba6feb6bda/static.py#L109

**POC**
Run a server

```
from static import Cling
from wsgiref.simple_server import make_server
my_app = Cling("/home/antitree/")
make_server("localhost", 9999, my_app).serve_forever()
```

Connect to a file that doesn't exist
`wget --server-response http://localhost:9999/TEST`

Response

```
Connecting to 127.0.0.1:9999... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 
  HTTP/1.0 404 Not Found
  Date: Sun, 07 Aug 2016 18:04:28 GMT
  Server: WSGIServer/0.2 CPython/3.5.2
  Content-type: text/plain
  Content-Length: 13
  Content-type: text/plain
```

Repeat, and second response

```
Connecting to 127.0.0.1:9999... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 
  HTTP/1.0 404 Not Found
  Date: Sun, 07 Aug 2016 18:04:28 GMT
  Server: WSGIServer/0.2 CPython/3.5.2
  Content-type: text/plain
  Content-Length: 13
  Content-type: text/plain
  Content-type: text/plain
```

Repeat 10,000 times to see that the headers are repeatedly added to.

issue_title: Downloading Attachments fails for a small number of datasets
issue_body:
This is only tested on nyc open data portal (https://nycopendata.socrata.com), but there are a handful of datasets for which the the attachment metadata either has no `assetId` property (see: `3av7-txd8`) or has an empty `assetId` property (see: `7isb-wh4c`). 

In either case, the path to the asset comes in the form `{base url}/api/assets/{blobId}?download=true` instead of `{base url}/api/views/{identifier}/files/{assetId}?download=true&filename={filename}`

If it has not `assetId property`, there is a Key Error and the client fails. If the `assetId` property is present, but empty, it appears that the `blobId` contains the asset ID, but since it is formatting the URL to a nonexistent resource, it is downloading an empty file.

issue_title: keyring leaks password into process table on OS X
issue_body:
Based on casual reading of the code in keyring/backends/OS_X.py, this code shells out to security(1), passing it the '-w password' option.  This leaks the password into the process table.

Unfortunately, security(1) does not provide any alternative methods of supplying the password, but it's possible to use a hack around it by creating a fifo, writing the password into the fifo and then using the shell's redirection mechanism to get input from the fifo to the command.

issue_title: SecretService cleanup
issue_body:
In [this comment](/kang/python-keyring-lib/pull-request/10/add-secret-service-backend#comment-792), Jonathan makes some good suggestions. Let's implement them.

*     Refactor the DBus initialization code to remove duplication.
*     Test a little bit more the supported() method. The existing implementation just checks one 'unsupported' case, and it's not even trivial by reading the test that it should be unsupported
*     Beware of BackendBasicTestCase, you *have* to overload the supported() method otherwise most of the useful tests will be completely skipped!
*     No need to return None in get_password()
*     Document the calls to 'assert prompt == "/"' (comment/assert message) so that we know what it is used for and if it failed, why is it important that it failed.

issue_title: Query active timer for context and python3 bytes to string issue
issue_body:
Hello Jesus,

2 issues here:

1)

I have taken a look through the source code and I am not sure if this is possible. I would like to be able to query whether there is an active "timer" running in a context. In the code below I have a rule which checks if the result is equal to failed, then it creates a context based increment counter, then below if the counter is equal to 3 perform an action.

However, I would like to evaluate whether the counter is equal to 3 AND there is a current timer running against this context. So something like this:

@when_all(all(s.failed_counter == 3, c.get_timer("failed_auth_time", c.s.sid))

So if there is a timer present it'll return as true.

```
from durable.lang import *
import datetime

with ruleset('auth'):
    # when first timer or less than 5 timeouts
    @when_all(m.result == "failed")
    def failed(c):
        try:
            c.s.failed_counter += 1
        except:
            c.s.failed_counter = 1
            c.start_timer('failed_auth_timer', 15, c.s.sid)

    @when_all(all(s.failed_counter == 3))
    def failed_threshold(c):
        print("did it work?")
        c.s.failed_counter = 0

    @when_all(timeout('failed_auth_timer'))
    def pulse(c):
        # We'll just let this die.
        print("{0} failed {1} times logging in".format(c.s.sid, c.s.failed_counter))
        print('pulse ->{0}'.format(datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%I:%M:%S%p')))

run_all()
```
I'd send 3 of these events within 15 seconds:

`curl -H "Content-type: application/json" -X POST -d '{"sid":"1", "result": "failed", "username": "thomas.knowles"}'  http://localhost:5000/auth/events`

2)

In python3 the REST API is not happy with the curl command above due to the data being interpreted as bytes as opposed to strings (the way python2 handles it) and requires myself to add ".decode("utf-8")" to the end of the following:

`message = json.loads(request.stream.read())`

So it looks like this:

`message = json.loads(request.stream.read().decode("utf-8") )`

issue_title: Memory leak in proximity.signed_distance
issue_body:
Hi,

I stumbled into this problem when trying to compute signed distance between a large set of points (> 1 million in my case) and a mesh. If I call 

`trimesh.proximity.signed_distance(mesh,points)`

the program will slowly eat all the computer's memory, going all the way up to 16 GB in my case. Instead, if I do : 

`for point in points:
    trimesh.proximity.signed_distance(mesh,[point])`

The program will run smoothly, with a stable memory consumption around 150 Mb (also related to other stuff running in my script). 

I'm not sure whether the term 'memory leak' is adequate, I didn't have time to look deeply into the problem. I suspect that there might be recursive calls which loop through the mesh and create a mess in the memory. That's probably not a big problem when not having a million points to compute, but suboptimal nonetheless.

issue_title: Security weight greater than 1.0
issue_body:
This may be similar to #67.
I was playing with get_transactions recently and observed something strange: there are spurious transactions for tiny amounts.
I'm testing a momentum strategy that selects the best one performing asset and allocates everything to it. (SelectN(1) then WeighEqually(), which I've verified does set a weight of exactly 1.0 for the desired security).

So the expectation is that every month, either nothing at all will happen (keeping the same security), or the existing position will be completely closed out and capital allocated to another security.
In other words, security weights are supposed to be 0 everywhere and exactly 1 elsewhere.

However, here are the weights reported by res.backtest['s1'].security_weights['GRE.PA']:
2012-10-01    1.008000
2012-10-02    1.007680
2012-10-03    1.007704
2012-10-04    1.007593
[...]

issue_title: Algorithm: DDS
issue_body:
The "Dynamically dimensioned search algorithm for computationally efficient watershed model calibration" algorithm by [Tolson & Shoemaker 2007](https://doi.org/10.1029/2005WR004723), looks like a good candidate for spotpy - especially for cases where a small number of model evaluation is more important than parameter precision. The paper compares SCE-UA with their new DDS algorithm and this comparison has been challanged in a commentary to that paper [Behrangi et al. 2008](https://doi.org/10.1029/2007WR006429) - however the commentary agrees on a faster yet not that exact convergance of the algorithm.

Implementation in R is available on GitHub: https://github.com/bdb67/Dynamically-Dimensioned-Search

issue_title: Bringing on a co-maintainer
issue_body:
As I'm returning from PyCon I'm realizing I've done a pretty bad job maintaining this project when there's clearly demand and interest.

Right now I don't have the time and mental energy to support this project as much as I'd like to (realpython.com keeps me plenty busy.)

So I'm looking to bring on one or more co-maintainers that can help me respond to open issues and PRs, and to help take this project into a sensible direction.

I've always believed that the main value of Schedule is simplicity. I'm looking for someone who gets that. I don't want to try to include everything and the kitchen sink into this project, and that's why I've resisted adding features like job persistence and so on (also, the PRs that proposed it so far have included shitty / fundamentally broken implementations.) 

The biggest danger I see to this project is turning it into a sprawling sea of half-assed features. I'm totally fine with pointing people to `cron` or `APScheduler` if it fits their needs better.

The person(s) I'm looking to bring on as a co-maintainer should be experienced enough to understand that. It also helps if you've been burned by overwhelming emerging complexity in your own projects ;-)

issue_title: We need a level normalisation convention
issue_body:
Folks, 

NM is MIDI compatible and uses 0..+64 range for unipolar k-rate signals, and -64...+64 for bipolar signals. 
The audio signals seem to be of the same level. 

Since there are lots of options, we should set a convention on levels. 
- We can have even 0dbfs = 64 
- We can normalise all signals to -64...64 / 0...64.
- On the other hand, it would be harder to combine pch2csd instruments with normal Csound way of coding in case of 64 based scaling
- We can do some re-scaling inside each UDO. But it literally means we have to do kOut /= 64 at the output and kIn *= 64 at the input layer of each UDO.

Any suggestions are welcome!!

issue_title: Return a pair from boltons.iterutils.bucketize
issue_body:
Hey there,

Reading the docs of this lib and wondering why not return a pair from `bucketize`?

Current behavior:

    >>> bucketize(range(5))
    {False: [0], True: [1, 2, 3, 4]}
    >>> is_odd = lambda x: x % 2 == 1
    >>> bucketize(range(5), is_odd)
    {False: [0, 2, 4], True: [1, 3]}

Instead:

    >>> bucketize(range(5))
    {False: [0], True: [1, 2, 3, 4]}

issue_title: Feature: Custom string format options
issue_body:
Python strings come with a builtin [`str.format`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format) method, which interprets the actual string as [format string](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#formatstrings) and uses data supplied through the `args` and `kwargs` arguments to fill in replacement fields.
gallery-dl utilizes this to build its directory- and filenames by applying extracted metadata to either default or [user-defined](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractorfilename) format strings.

To allow developers to customize and extend the format string syntax, Python also provides a [Formatter class](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#custom-string-formatting), which offers the same functionality as `str.format` and also enables developers to overwrite and extend its methods and functionality. Using this class comes at the cost of performance: `str.format` is at least 30 times faster than `string.Formatter.format`.

With c1f0afe4c6ce94c7c09cd725b7066da54075bc9d I've taken the code of `string.Formatter` and stripped it down to a bare minimum, while keeping all functionality necessary for gallery-dl intact and optimizing it for performance. This made it only 5 times slower than `str.format` and also enabled same extra formatting options:

- [Conversions](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3101/#explicit-conversion-flag)
  - `l`: converts all characters to lowercase (`abc`)
  - `u`: converts all characters to uppercase (`ABC`)
  - `c`: capitalizes the first letter and lowercases the rest (`Abc`)
  - `C`: calls `string.capwords`, which performs the same operation as `c` on each word (`Abc Def`)

- [Format Specifiers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3101/#format-specifiers)
  - `?<before>/<after>/`: Adds `<before>` and `<after>` to the actual value if it evaluates to `True`, otherwise the whole replacement field becomes an empty string. This allows to add some extra characters before/after optional keyword values.
        Example: `{f:?-+/+-/}` -> `"-+Example+-"`, if `f` contains `"Example"` or `""` if `f` is empty
        See also: https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/82ea6c0cd396237ca81f607ba018658085132348/gallery_dl/extractor/imgur.py#L55


Todo:
- [x] Improve Python's custom string formatter
- [x] Implement basic custom formatting features
- [ ] Additional formatting features (?)
- [ ] Proper documentation

If anyone has any other ideas for useful formatting options, let me know.

issue_title: New operation to update an array's value
issue_body:
I think it'd be useful to have an updating operation to append to an array element. I've tried this successfully:

```python
def append(field, value):
    """
    Append a given value to a given array field.
    """
    def transform(element):
        element[field].append(value)

    return transform
```

Can be used as:

```python
db.update(append("some_array_field","value_to_append"), eids=[1,2])
```

Perhaps it needs some try/catch in case the element is not an array and the value can't be appended.

issue_title: Lack of ISO 8601 date if we use options
issue_body:
Continuing the last thought...

Without options we get a summary of information and an ISO 8601 date.

```
%watermark

2016-01-31T21:17:31

CPython 3.4.4
IPython 4.0.1

compiler   : GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)
system     : Linux
release    : 3.16.0-38-generic
machine    : x86_64
processor  : x86_64
CPU cores  : 8
interpreter: 64bit
```

If we add extra options, we lose the ISO 8601 date-time (and just have the date):
```
%watermark -d -m -v -p numpy,matplotlib -g

2016-01-31 

CPython 3.4.4
IPython 4.0.1

numpy 1.10.2
matplotlib 1.5.0

compiler   : GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)
system     : Linux

processor  : x86_64
CPU cores  : 8
interpreter: 64bit

Could we either have a new flag for an ISO 8601 date, perhaps ```-i``` which uses the same new ```strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S')```?

issue_title: How to include/reference complex data structures/objects?
issue_body:
This is a great tool but it seems to lack (at least documentation about) a way to include/interpolate nested structures, e.g. I have some default values (in my case, AWS Cloudformation Tag definitions) that I need insert a bunch of times at different nesting levels.  A more simple example might look something like this:

`defaultprefs.yaml`
```yaml
    pref1: 1
    pref2: 2
```
`stream.yaml`
```yaml
user1:
    prefs: {% include 'defaultprefs.yaml' %}
user2:
    prefs: {% include 'defaultprefs.yaml' %}
---
user1:
    prefs:
        pref2: override2
user2:
    prefs:
        pref3: 3
```

With a deep-merge output of:

```yaml
user1:
    prefs:
        pref1: 1
        pref2: override2
user2:
    prefs:
        pref1: 1
        pref2: 2
        pref3: 3
```

Obviously, the order that hiyapyco interpolates the jinja2 doesn't let this work (I'd have to wrap the `{` character in quotes to at least make the yaml parseable) but it gives an idea of the functionality. A more realistic solution would be similar to the `!include` call described [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/528281/how-can-i-include-an-yaml-file-inside-another).

I assume it's possible to override/extend the `ODYLDo` but it would be nice if it could do this on its own.

issue_title: W602 breaks three-argument raise
issue_body:
autopep8 tries to convert "raise a, b, c" into "raise a(b, c)", but that's incorrect.  "raise a(b), None, c" would be correct in some cases but not all (depending on the values of a and b).  I don't think there's a guaranteed-safe way to make this transformation statically, so it's better to leave three-argument raise alone.

Sample code:
https://github.com/facebook/tornado/blob/master/tornado/web.py#L977

issue_title: Parameters start with error are processed as code block.
issue_body:
We have following docstring:
---
"""
        Wrapper for @ref wait_until_state_reached, for cases when the intended call is for that marker to be reached

        Args:
            marker_extensions (dict): Extra content for the marker

            marker_name (str) : Identifier of the marker.

            name (str):  Key of marker content. If empty string, then the content will be an empty dict

            timeout (int):  the max amount of seconds to wait for this marker to be reached.
            
            error_contents (list of dict) : List of markers treated as error condition, causing the
                           method to return false if one was found.
                           Content of dict {"\<MarkerType\>": {\<Marker Content\>}}
        """
---
the processing results in:
---
    ## @brief         Wrapper for @ref wait_until_state_reached, for cases when the intended call is for that marker to be reached
    #
    #
    # @param            marker_extensions       Extra content for the marker
    #
    # @param            marker_name     Identifier of the marker.
    #
    # @param            name    Key of marker content. If empty string, then the content will be an empty dict
    #
    # @param            timeout the max amount of seconds to wait for this marker to be reached.
    #            error_contents (list of dict) : List of markers treated as error condition, causing the
    #                           method to return false if one was found.
    #                           Content of dict {"\<MarkerType\>": {\<Marker Content\>}}
    #
---

witch forces doxygen to put "error_contents ....." into a code block.
we expect a @param here.

Maybe the regex "__errorLineRE" have to be fine tuned.

issue_title: Issue with Property Name Capitalization
issue_body:
I've worked up a MWE of something that looks like a bug. I've got a very simple LabVIEW vi, and a very simple Python script. Either file can write a simple numerical array to a tdms file, with a couple of channel-level properties. It appears that when LabVIEW writes a channel, there are three automatic channel-level properties that LabVIEW writes on-the-fly: NI_ChannelLength, NI_DataType, and name. These three data pieces seem to be there whether I open the tdms file in the little native LabVIEW TDMS file viewer, or in Scout, or in DIAdem, or if I import it into Excel using the add-in. However, the npTDMS Python module never returns these three pieces of data in the TdmsFile.object('Group', 'Channel').properties data. I understand these data pieces might be generated on-the-fly, perhaps only when you read the file. Shouldn't the npTDMS module do the same?

[Test npTDMS.zip](https://github.com/adamreeve/npTDMS/files/1589861/Test.npTDMS.zip)

issue_title: support 'Signature' HTTP header?
issue_body:
hi! first off, thanks for maintaining and working on this library. we appreciate it!

i'm implementing a protocol ([ActivityPub](https://w3c.github.io/activitypub/)) and using HTTP Signatures via the [`Signature` HTTP header](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-cavage-http-signatures-07#section-4), but httpsig currently only supports the `Authorization` header. any chance you could add `Signature` support?

as a proof of concept, i've done this in snarfed/httpsig@6f0ebfe00af39fb75d50c7a29f46da49e8190d70. example usage in snarfed/bridgy-fed@b1230881588b9d990db9fa13f13376b5624c69e6. it's definitely not the way you'd do it for real, but it does work.

thanks in advance!

issue_title: jedi-0.10.1 release on pypi is missing grammar*.txt
issue_body:
Possibly related to #904? I found this while trying to track down the same issue on my laptop.

Both the wheel `jedi-0.10.1-py2.py3-none-any.whl` and sdist tarball `jedi-0.10.1.tar.gz` on [jedi 0.10.1's pypi page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/jedi) are missing all the grammar files such as `grammar2.7.txt` and `grammar3.6.txt`.

This seems to have happened despite the fact that `MANIFEST.in` has the line `include jedi/parser/grammar*.txt`.

To find this, I attached strace to the anaconda-mode process which Spacemacs had spawned for syntax checking. The strace output from just after the anaconda-mode process received an RPC from Spacemacs contains very many repetitions of:

issue_title: Memory leak in from_string
issue_body:
udatetime version 0.13 leaks memory when calling from_string.

Example (Python 2.7):

```python
import udatetime

s = u"2008-09-08T22:47:31-07:00"
while True:
    t = udatetime.from_string(s)
```
The pure python version of from_string does not leak, only the C-extension (from_rfc3339_string) does.

issue_title: nbstripout crashes when input is empty
issue_body:
**Steps to reproduce**

    $ cat /dev/null | nbstripout

**Expected results**

Zero bytes of output; exit code 0.

**Actual results**

Stack trace; exit code 1. E.g.:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  [...]
json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  [...]
nbformat.reader.NotJSONError: Notebook does not appear to be JSON: ''...
```

**Comments**

Version 0.3.1 on OS X, installed with Conda.

This make it harder to recover from bad Git commits, e.g. caused by #55, because Git won't do anything unless its filters exit successfully. One must disable the filter, untangle things, and then re-enable it.

Thank you!

issue_title: implement square, real
issue_body:
I haven't found a way to compute the squared norm of a quaternion array. I can use `np.square(np.norm(q))` but this sound inefficient to me. If the function exists I would like to see an example usage in the documentation. Also I would like to take the real part of a quaternion: so that I could be able to write `sqr_q = (q * q.conj).real` but `.real` gives the whole quaternion back. Similarly it could be useful if `.imag` gives the imaginary parts (as a 3D vector).

Suggested behaviour:  np.square, np.real, np.imag to be extended to act on quaternions

issue_title: Multiplication with scalar depend on order
issue_body:
Order of multiplication of scalar and quaternion matters. It works fine from right:

    >>> q = np.quaternion(1,2,3,4)
    >>> q * 5
    quaternion(5, 10, 15, 20)

But not from left:

    >>> 5 * q
    TypeError: Input object is not a quaternion.

Even worse is that if you do this with an array of quaternions, there is no warning, but the result seems to be more or less random.

    >>> q * np.arange(5)
    array([quaternion(0, 0, 0, 0), quaternion(1, 2, 3, 4),
       quaternion(2, 4, 6, 8), quaternion(3, 6, 9, 12),
       quaternion(4, 8, 12, 16)], dtype=quaternion)

   >>> np.arange(5) * q
   array([quaternion(4, 8, 12, 16), quaternion(0, 0, 0, 0),
       quaternion(0, 0, 0, 0), quaternion(0, 0, 0, 0),
       quaternion(0, 0, 0, 0)], dtype=quaternion)

issue_title: Commands: only /start available to pass argument?
issue_body:
Could I pass arguments to different command?
If it is not possible, how can I authenticate user and authorize him at 3rd party service via oAuth2?
For example, check out: Youtube bot or Github. 
I also noticed that they redirect to https://integrations.telegram.me, but I still can't understand how exactly they authenticate user as far as oAuth2 doesn't contain any info about user who grants permission

I need to authorize user at 3rd party services. For example, Google calendar. 
So, I decided to create simple URI that redirects to Service Sign In and redirect URL to my server with token\authCode. 
As far as oauth does not authenticate user, I still need somehow identify who exactly granted access to his resources.
So my next logical step was to hash received token and send it back to user via https://telegram.me/BOT?code=xxx 
I was convinced that if there is commandHandler for /code and /code is in the bot commands list I would be able to open conversation with my bot and sent this hashed code via webhook back to my server in order to detect who exactly it was at access grant step.
I was shocked when I found that my plan was ruined at the last step: as far as I can see there is only /start command could be triggerred. 
My question is: can you confirm that only /start command could query parameters via URL? if so, could you give me some advise about the right way of authorizing and authenticationg user?

issue_title: cache_auth file is world readable (security concern)
issue_body:
discord.py caches a token for server logins. On my Linux system the cache file is in `/tmp/discord_py` and both the directory and the file are world readable. That means anyone with access to my Linux system could use my Discord login's credentials.

I think the solution is to change [the code that creates the cache file](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/async/discord/client.py#L200-L203) to make the file only readable by the owner. It'd be nice to use the `tempfile` module in Python to create the file, but I'm not sure it's quite suitable. Using `os.chmod()` to make the file mode 0600 should work but then that needs a bit of testing on non-Unix platforms.

I'm filing this issue now to document it. I should be able to contribute a pull request to close it, but it may take a few days.

issue_title: Non-required key raises key error when not present
issue_body:
In serpy 0.2, if a field set to `required=False` is not present in the object to be serialized, it will raise a `KeyError`. Based on my understanding of how DRF works, if a field set to `required=False` has a missing key, it will just skip it and move on. 

http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/fields/#required

issue_title: Error logging is missing info
issue_body: Exceptions are generally logged using `%s` (str representation), but the default exception str output only includes the arguments used to create the exception. That causes many errors to only include the args and not the error type [example: `ConnectionError(conn)` would be logged with only the `conn` but not ConnectionError. BrokerResponseError adds a custom `__str__` method that includes the class name, but it does not include the args. So `GroupCoordinatorNotAvailableError(group_id)` would log a string showing the error type but not the specific group id.

issue_title: Client never chooses IPv6 after bootstrapping
issue_body:
If you provide IPv6 addresses when creating the client (e.g., `bootstrap_servers=["[::1]:9092"]`) and the server responds with a hostname (e.g., `localhost`), this library always actually uses IPv4. This is because the `get_ip_port_afi` function, when passed a hostname, always returns and address family of `AF_INET`.

Ideally, I think this code should be restructured to use `getaddrinfo` and a loop to detect whether the host is available over v4 or v6. It looks like there's no actual resolution in `kafka/conn.py`; we're relying on implicit resolution done by passing a hostname to `socket.connect_ex`; hopefully that uses getaddrinfo behind the scenes and follows RFC 3484.

issue_title: migrate COT to support Travis-CI container infrastructure
issue_body:
We should be able to enhance the speed of our Travis-CI builds by migrating to the newer infrastructure: http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/migrating-from-legacy/

In order to accomplish this migration, instead of using 'cot install-helpers' to install the helper tools (which requires 'sudo', which is only available in the legacy infra), we need replicate the helper installtion into our .travis.yml.

Specifically we need to use `addons.apt.packages` in our travis.yml (http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/migrating-from-legacy/#Adding-APT-Packages) to install all of the packages that are available through apt-get, then build and locally install any that are not.

apt-get packages:
* `make`
* `gcc`
* `genisoimage` (travis-ci/apt-package-whitelist#593)
* `qemu-utils` (travis-ci/apt-package-whitelist#553)
* `zlib1g-dev`

packages to build from source:
* `fatdisk`
* `vmdktool`

issue_title: Add ability to downoad other routeview archives
issue_body: This issue is a follow up on adding IPv6 functionality. The module now supports IPv6 if the IPASN file given to it has IPv6 prefixes. The pyasn_util_convert can convert/create this if the given MRT-RIB has IPv6 prefixes. pyasn_util_download currently downloads routeview-1 archives which only have IPv4, and the BGP dumps with IPv4/IPv6 prefixes to be downloaded manually (see: www.routeviews.org). It might be good to add an option to add files routeviews-4. The prerequisite is to understand the differences between the routes advertised in these different dumps more clearly to make sure changing the input has no adverse effects.

issue_title: Unhandled exception when redirect without Location header is scanned
issue_body:
An unhandled exception occurs when a server responds with a redirect, but the first response packet does not contain a "Location" HTTP header.  This happens with any of the standard scanner plugins.

Output:
Unhandled exception when processing --tlsv1:
TypeError - cannot concatenate 'str' and 'NoneType' objects

Reason in /utils/ssl_connection.py:
```python
if 300 <= http_response.status < 400:
    # Add redirection URL to the result
    redirect = ' - ' + http_response.getheader('Location', None)
```

If a redirect response code is received, but there isn't a Location header in the first packet, then this particular getheader() call returns None, leading to the exception.

Recommended fix:
```python
if 300 <= http_response.status < 400:
    if http_response.getheader('Location', None):
        # Add redirection URL to the result
        redirect = ' - ' + http_response.getheader('Location', None)
```

I'll submit a pull request.

issue_title: Rlens geometry problem
issue_body:
I have forked TreeCorr to try to implement Rlens myself, which I have so far done by copying the Rperp code, the only differences being how DistSq() is defined and how we handle TooSmallDist() and TooLargeDist(). However, I am (embarrassingly) having a very hard time with the geometry of finding Rlens with which to write DistSq().

Knowing that we want to find an expression for Rlens^2 only in terms of r1, r2 and r (i.e. distance to position1, 2 and their separation) and NOT in terms of any trig functions (since those are computationally slow) it boils down to solving the following geometry problem:
[Rlens_triangle.pdf](https://github.com/rmjarvis/TreeCorr/files/189801/Rlens_triangle.pdf)

Rlens is labeled L, and r1, r2 and r are labeled a, b, and c respectively. The solution that myself and Erika (Eduardo's other grad student) arrived at was the following (mathematica assisted):

(L^2 + a^2) * b^2 = (a^2 + Sqrt(c^2*a^2 + c^2*L^2 -a^2*L^2))^2.

Unfortunately of the four solutions to this, none of them are real (for example L = +-ia, and the other two are similar).

Perhaps you have some insight into this, and have done the geometry already. I am worried that if we write DistSq() for Rlens with trig functions involved then TreeCorr will get heavily bogged down. Let me know what you think!

issue_title: On BooleanAlgebra and design
issue_body:
@bastikr It dawned on me that there is a little used object called BooleanAlgebra?

I am not sure I grok entirely its purpose, but it seems to have been designed as a central configuration and entry point.
In particular it holds the base elements for `TRUE` and `FALSE` and the default `Symbol` class.
I wonder if we should use this class more and instead of passing a `symbol_class` to `parse`, passing it an algebra.
Also we could have it hold the strings to use for `__str__` and `__repr__` for operators, parens, etc. I very much want and need to have  custom serializations that would not use the default `*+~`.
Also the parse function could become also a method of this?

So a custom algebra is something where you could redefine what is the parsing or tokenization logic,what are the base elements, the Symbol class to use and how expressions are serialized. 

I am just throwing out some ideas, but there is a few quirks in the current design that tickle me.

@Kronuz you take is appreciated too

issue_title: Support keyboard media keys (for brightness and volume)
issue_body:
I use `backlight` and `volume_status`, but even though both of them have functionality to change the value, it is only accessible via mouse click/scroll, not via keyboard.

My keyboard has dedicated media buttons for adjusting brightness and volume, I want to use them, it is faster than reaching to the mouse.

That's why I currently don't use functionality from the py3status module, but instead I reimplemented the functionality myself and have the following config in my `.i3/config`:

```
bindsym XF86AudioMute         exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-volume.sh mute
bindsym XF86AudioRaiseVolume  exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-volume.sh +5
bindsym XF86AudioLowerVolume  exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-volume.sh -5
bindsym XF86MonBrightnessUp   exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-brightness.sh inc
bindsym XF86MonBrightnessDown exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-brightness.sh dec
```

Two problems with this:

- This prevents code sharing and collaboration. Today every person who wants to use keyboard has to stop using py3status for adjusting the values and reimplement the same logic in their own scripts.
- The scripts that I made cannot refresh only one module, they have to issue `killall -USR1 py3status`, but this is very inefficient and when I press e.g. "volume down" button several times repeatedly, my CPU usage reaches 100%.

-------

We can of course extend this to a more generic "support any keyboard shortcuts", but I decided to start with this smaller and more specific example.

issue_title: -r / --rcfile / rcfile option: Path including space char does not work on Windows
issue_body:
## Problem Description ##

The [MeCab 0.996 option flags](http://mecab.googlecode.com/svn/trunk/mecab/doc/mecab.html) ```-r``` / ```--rcfile``` / ```rcfile``` for specifying a resource file will not work via natto-py when the path is a Windows path including whitespace chars.

## Steps to reproduce the problem ##

1. Install MeCab 0.996.exe on Windows XP
2. Install Python 2.7.8
3. Install natto-py 0.0.2
4. Execute the following:
```
#!python
import natto
import os

os.environ['MECAB_PATH']='C:/Program Files/MeCab/bin/libmecab.dll'
os.environ['MECAB_CHARSET']='shift-jis'

nm = natto.MeCab("-rC:\Program Files\MeCab\etc\mymecabrc")
```

* Observe that the above code results in the following exception being thrown:

```
#!python

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "natto\api.py", line 273, in __init__
    raise MeCabError(self._ERROR_INIT % str(verr))
natto.api.MeCabError: Could not initialize MeCab: unrecognized arguments: -rC:\Program Files\MeCab\etc\mymecabrc
```

## Expected behavior ##

The reference to mecab via natto-py should be successfully instantiated.

Note that using ```-r``` / ```--rcfile``` from the mecab command-line will work by simply surrounding the Windows path with double-quotes.

```
#!script

mecab -r"C:\Program Files\MeCab\etc\mymecabrc"
```

## Version, platform, environment details ##

1. MeCab 0.996.exe
2. Windows XP Professional, Version 2002, Service Pack 3
3. Python 2.7.8
4. natto-py 0.0.2


Originally  opened 2014-11-05.
Archiving this issue for historical reasons.

issue_title: Variable Sizer
issue_body:
Hi,

I think it would be great if by default a kind of variable sizer (even a simple one, if simple it can be) could be present in backtrader. As far as I understand, sizers is a big piece of work in perspective.

Right now it is only possible to call buy or sell orders specifying a fix amount of shares (buy X shares or sell Y shares), but a common practice is to call an order for a given amount of money (buy for X' dollars). With a fix size, the strategy can ask to the broker to buy X shares, but when it comes to the broker to accomplish the order the price may have increased. As a consequence, the available cash may not be enough to buy the required X shares and the order is dismissed/rejected.

Is  such implementation feasible? If yes, is it planned in a near future, or will it be implemented along with more other complex sizers?

Regards,

Julien

issue_title: cannot cast to string a StdinDataRedirection-bound LocalCommand
issue_body:
Having directed string input into the standard input of a `LocalCommand` (via `<<` *i.e.* `__lshift__`), I am unable to cast the resulting `StdinDataRedirection` object to string. Attempting to do so raises an uncaught exception.

    $ ipython
    Python 3.6.3 (default, Dec 20 2017, 11:51:43) 
    Type 'copyright', 'credits' or 'license' for more information
    IPython 6.2.1 -- An enhanced Interactive Python. Type '?' for help.
    
    In [1]: import plumbum
    
    In [2]: plumbum.__version__
    Out[2]: (1, 6, 4)
    
    In [3]: cat = plumbum.local['cat']
    
    In [4]: stdin_bound_cat = cat << 'hello world'
    
    In [5]: str(stdin_bound_cat)
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
    <ipython-input-37-15af60a0e450> in <module>()
    ----> 1 str(stdin_bound_cat)
    
    ~/.pyenv/versions/3.6.3/envs/test/lib/python3.6/site-packages/plumbum/commands/base.py in __str__(self)
         49
         50     def __str__(self):
    ---> 51         return " ".join(self.formulate())
         52
         53     def __or__(self, other):
    
    TypeError: sequence item 2: expected str instance, list found

Underlying this issue is that the `formulate()` method for this object returns a list which itself contains both strings and lists:

    In [6]: stdin_bound_cat.formulate()
    Out[6]: ["echo 'hello world'", '|', ['/bin/cat']]

which result cannot simply be joined by a string, as its `__str__` attempts to do.

Needless to say, this issue prevents passing such objects to `print()`, and minorly complicates introspection.

issue_title: Feature request: Thread safety and SharedStrings
issue_body:
So, i decided to generate multiple sheets of one workbook in separate threads and i've discovered that if i use `in_memory` option `XlsxWriter` will use `SharedStrings`, which is not thread safe at all. 

Am i correct with my assumptions? And if so, can we add mutex (using some option maybe)? Something like:

``` python
# A metadata class to store Excel strings between worksheets.
class SharedStringTable(object):
    """
    A class to track Excel shared strings between worksheets.

    """

    def __init__(self):
        self.count = 0
        self.unique_count = 0
        self.string_table = {}
        self.string_array = []
        self._lock = Lock()

    def _get_shared_string_index(self, string):
        """" Get the index of the string in the Shared String table. """
        try:
            self._lock.acquire()
            if string not in self.string_table:
                # String isn't already stored in the table so add it.
                index = self.unique_count
                self.string_table[string] = index
                self.count += 1
                self.unique_count += 1
                return index
            else:
                # String exists in the table.
                index = self.string_table[string]
                self.count += 1
                return index
        finally:
            self._lock.release()

    def _get_shared_string(self, index):
        """" Get a shared string from the index. """
        try:
            self._lock.acquire()
            return self.string_array[index]
        finally:
            self._lock.release()

    def _sort_string_data(self):
        """" Sort the shared string data and convert from dict to list. """
        try:
            self._lock.acquire()
            self.string_array = sorted(self.string_table,
                                       key=self.string_table.__getitem__)
            self.string_table = {}
        finally:
            self._lock.release()

    def _get_strings(self):
        """" Return the sorted string list. """
        try:
            self._lock.acquire()
            return self.string_array
        finally:
            self._lock.release()
```

And is there anything else in `XlsxWriter` that i need to worry about?

issue_title: Proposal: allow `javascript_tag_for(*names)` to accept names not matching `\.js$`
issue_body: Currently, `javascript_tag(*names)` assumes all JS asset bundles have a `.js` suffix  (see [L91](https://github.com/nickjj/flask-webpack/blob/master/flask_webpack/__init__.py#L91)) . This causes `javascript_tag('my_asset')` to look for `my_asset.js` while the manifest only maps `"my_asset": "my_bundle.js"`.  This writes  nothing to the template.  I'd propose looking up `my_asset` and falling back to looking up `my_asset.js`.

issue_title: Horizontal Privilege scalation - Download certificates without privs
issue_body:
Hi,
If you create staff users on Django. Even if they don't have enough privileges to access to the CAs, it's possible to download the CA Certs in PEM format, for example through this URL:

* /admin/django_ca/certificateauthority/1/download/?format=PEM

**Steps to replicate**
1. Create a django project and add django-ca.
2. Add django.admin and synchronize the database.
2. Create the initial CA and superuser
3. Login onto the admin portal as admin
4. Create a user and set as "staff"
5. Login with this user. It won't have enough privs to access to anything by default. But...
6. Open the following URL:
 * /admin/django_ca/certificateauthority/1/download/?format=PEM
The Certificate of the CA will be downloaded.

issue_title: Fix issue on Ackley Function
issue_body:
* PySwarms version: 0.1.5
* Python version: 2.7
* Operating System: Linux

### Description

It seems that the Ackley function is not working on Python 2.7 version

```python
def ackley_func(x):
    if not np.logical_and(x >= -32, x <= 32).all():
        raise ValueError('Input for Ackley function must be within [-32, 32].')

    d = x.shape[1]
    j = (-20.0 * np.exp(-0.2 * np.sqrt((1/d) * (x**2).sum(axis=1)))
        - np.exp((1/float(d)) * np.cos(2 * np.pi * x).sum(axis=1))
        + 20.0
        + np.exp(1))

    return j
```

When given an input `np.zeros([3,2])`, most Python 3.X versions return something that `isclose` to `np.zeros(3)`. But when ran against a Python 2.7.X version, we're getting `[1.718, 1.718, 1.718]`

issue_title: tkintertable error when creates .EXE file with cx_Freeze
issue_body:
Hi everyone, 

I have found a bug when I try to create cx_freeze .exe file.
For example, I have 2 files: `app.py` and `setup.py`

Inside of app.py

```
from Tkinter import *
from tkFileDialog import askopenfilename
from tkintertable.Tables import TableCanvas
from tkintertable.TableModels import TableModel
.
.
.

window = Tk()

window.mainloop()

```
When I compile this using python it runs perfectly.
Notice that I have only called the library and I am not using it.
Now I want to create an `.EXE` file using cx_Freeze.

```
from cx_Freeze import setup,Executable

includes = []
excludes = []
packages = []
filename = "app.py"

exe = Executable(
   script = "app.py",
   base = 'Win32GUI',
   targetName = "app.exe",
   icon = 'icon.ico'
)

setup(
    name = 'App',
    version = '0.1',
    description = 'App description',
    author = 'author',
    author_email = 'mail@mail.com',
    options = {'build_exe': {'excludes':excludes,'packages':packages,'includes':includes}},
    executables = [exe])

```

The error occurs when I open the `.exe` file. This happens: 

![capture](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13356409/31829876-308fe00a-b584-11e7-86a8-8c1f820ca50c.PNG)

There are an error with PMW loader. 

I really dont know what I have to do. Is it PMW error? or is a tkintertable bug?

Thanks.

issue_title: Suppress initial call to st-pipe function / best way to handle url param for filter
variable precedence
issue_body:
I think it would be useful to take a look at how variable precedence is being evaluated. 

The current hierarchy seems to be: template > cli param > input file
I would suggest instead: cli param > input file > template

The suggested order would give more priority to specifically defined variables and less priority to more generally defined (template-level) vars.

issue_title: Reversion Middleware issue when migrating to Django 2
issue_body:
Hi,

When migrating from the `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` django setting to the `MIDDLEWARE` setting, django calls `__init__` and `__call__` methods on the middleware instead of `process_request` and `process_response`.
In the case of `RevisionMiddleware`, the behavior is different when we use the `MIDDLEWARE` setting instead of the `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` one, because the [create_revision in the views](https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/blob/51abcac5db7f3b73eb1503c3bcf591b9f7240523/reversion/views.py#L22) rolls back when the status code of the http request > 400, however the old behavior uses the [create_revision context manager](https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/blob/51abcac5db7f3b73eb1503c3bcf591b9f7240523/reversion/revisions.py#L297).
One is used in the `__init__` and the other is used in the `process_request` in the [RevisionMiddleware](https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/blob/master/reversion/middleware.py#L6).

What I see is that the migration from one setting to the other is not consistent, which makes the behavior with Django 2 different.

Is this intended to be different?

issue_title: Share model registration between multiple RevisionManagers
issue_body:
Hi.

I'm currently implementing a RevisionManager that will allow the 'before' revision to be saved, by hooking into pre_save instead of post_save. I would like to provide a way to link this manager to the default one (or any other 'normal' manager), so that they will share exactly the same model registration, to avoid having to add a duplicate call to register() my manager for every model.

I currently have them sharing the same _registered_models dictionary, but this doesn't connect up the signals. I have a few ideas, but all of them have downsides:
1. Change all models to call my register() method and pass that to the default manager (undesirable, I would like to avoid having to change the models if possible)
2. Provide some sort of hook into RevisionManager.register() to call my register() method (where? Need to guarantee that no models have been registered)
3. Wait until all models have been registered and then add signals in an iterator (where? Need to guarantee that all models have been registered)
4. Replace default_revision_manager with some sort of stack that managers can be subscribed to?

Please share if you have any thoughts on how I should or shouldn't go about doing this.

Thanks.
Simon

issue_title: Support composite indices on foreign key columns
issue_body: Currently the function `has_index` returns `True` correctly when the column is the first in an index, but  `is_indexed_foreign_key` requires the sets of columns in the key and an index to match fully. The correct behavior, as I understand, is to return `True` in the foreign key columns are at the start of any index (and in any order).

issue_title: get_occurrences does not respect timezone across DST boundaries
issue_body:
It seems that there is no way to generate occurrences that are timezone-aware; for example, if a user creates an Event with a weekly recurrence rule and start = "2015-03-04T09:00:00-08:00" (9am PST), the generated occurrence for 2015-03-11 will be 1 hour off because dateutil.rrule does not support timezone in its icalendar implementation.

Has anybody come across this and found a solution that doesn't require forking dateutil or modifying the underlying ical implementation?

issue_title: possibility to lock user / set user status
issue_body:
Current user status is visible at `status`, and it's also possible to filter users by status using
```py
redmine.user.filter(status=…)
```

The meaning of each number is also documented at `docs/resources/user.rst`.

Bot it seems like it's currently not possible to update a users status through the API (which is possible using the PUT method on `/users/{id}.json` with data
```json
'{"user": {"status": 3}}')`

issue_title: Integrate with --capture and -v options of pytest
issue_body:
There's a [pytest-logging](https://github.com/saltstack/pytest-logging) plugin by @s0undt3ch that provides a nice way to control logging through the default `-v` and `-s` (alias for `--capture=no`) options of pytest-core. The verbosity flag naturally configures the logging level, and the capturing option - whether to output live logs for all tests, or only capture them to show in case of a failure afterwards.

For instance, one can run `py.test -vv -s` to see live logs at the `INFO` level or above emitted by all tests.

It would be nice to have something like that in pytest-catchlog.

issue_title: html.render() always create new browser instance
issue_body:
Example: 
```python
from requests_html import HTMLSession
session = HTMLSession()
r = session.get('http://python-requests.org/')
r.html.render()
r = session.get('http://python-requests.org/')
r.html.render()
```
Output:
[I:pyppeteer.launcher] Browser listening on: ws://127.0.0.1:52135/devtools/browser/dd157ee2-199c-4477-9c80-94d19467eee0
[I:pyppeteer.launcher] Browser listening on: ws://127.0.0.1:34303/devtools/browser/c5a4ad8f-8e38-4113-8443-c7619d9befca

With every time when render is called:
```python
browser = await pyppeteer.launch(headless=True, args=['--no-sandbox'])
page = await browser.newPage()
```
It makes render not usable at all..

First proposal:
```python
r.html.render(new_browser=True)
r.html.render()
```
Second proposal:
- add browser object to api and then 
```python
r.browser.new()  # creates new browser
r.browser.render()  #renders current page
```

future
i18n
l10n
log
rest_framework
sass_tags
static
staticfiles
tz
```

The application uses Django REST framework, but not django-crispy-forms (it's an optional dependency). I figured out that django-sass-processor loads all templates to look for `sass_src` tags ([sass_processor/management/commands/compilescss.py#L85](https://github.com/jrief/django-sass-processor/blob/master/sass_processor/management/commands/compilescss.py#L85)). This means mean the django-crispy-forms templates included with Django REST framework also get loaded even if we're not using them.

After thinking for a while I thought a template blacklist would be a good solution. What do you think about it?

Thanks,
Frank

issue_title: Reloading matplotlib overwrites custom settings
issue_body:
Hi it seems like reloading matplotlib using ```imp``` as you do on line 81 in ```scalebar.py``` causes some issues with custom settings.
For example:

```python
from matplotlib_scalebar.scalebar import ScaleBar
from matplotlib_scalebar.dimension import _Dimension
import matplotlib as mpl

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
mpl.rcParams.update({'font.family': 'serif'})
```
will lead to 
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10776763/23097117/3538f186-f62c-11e6-8da6-f50ad1e0b746.png)

As you can see the fonts are sans-serif and not serif.
Doing the same without importing matplotlib_scalebar, will lead to the expected result:

```python
# from matplotlib_scalebar.scalebar import ScaleBar
# from matplotlib_scalebar.dimension import _Dimension
import matplotlib as mpl

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
mpl.rcParams.update({'font.family': 'serif'})
```
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10776763/23097125/7556059c-f62c-11e6-869b-0d1c30012cc5.png)

By playing around with a local install of your package I could verify, that reloading matplotlib (line 81) causes this issue.
```python
imp.reload(sys.modules['matplotlib'])
```
Removing that line resolves the issue and does not seem to change anything else. (all test still work and all other things I tested, too)
Could you explain what the original intention of this line was?

Best regards,
Arne

(PS: awesome package otherwise! Saved me a lot of time!)

issue_title: Why revoke tokens functions are no more available ?
refresh tokens doesn't carry the user claims
issue_body:
I want to use the user claims to hold a list of allowed roles for the user, as outlined in https://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tokens_from_complex_object.html. Login works, the access token contains the roles. The protected routes get access to that list of roles from the token as per https://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/complex_objects_from_token.html, so that part works.

Come token refresh time (https://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/refresh_tokens.html): The token refresh endpoint is protected with the refresh token, so has access to the refresh token and not the (usually expired, at that point) access token. It tries to build a new access token. The refresh token holds the subject identity, but *not* the user claims. Thus, an extra database read (or whatever code depending on the user store) is required to rebuild the role list.

I have no idea wether the refresh token not carrying the user claims is a design/security decision. If it is, so be it -- I can live with an extra database lookup for each token refresh. But if it is not, it would be convenient to be able to write a function that build a new access token from the refresh token only; For that to be possible, the  refresh token should carry the user claims.

issue_title: Refresh token with complex object
issue_body:
Hello everyone,

I am trying to do is refresh token with complex object. My code is as follows:
```python

from flask import Blueprint, jsonify, request
from flask_jwt_extended import jwt_required, create_access_token, \
    jwt_refresh_token_required, create_refresh_token,\
    get_jwt_identity, get_jwt_claims
from utility import to_dict
from my_app.auth.models import User
from my_app import app
from flask_jwt_extended import JWTManager


auth = Blueprint("auth", __name__)
jwt = JWTManager(app)


# This is an example of a complex object that we could build
# a JWT from. In practice, this will likely be something
# like a SQLAlchemy instance.
class UserObject:
    def __init__(self, username, roles):
        self.username = username
        self.roles = roles


# Create a function that will be called whenever create_access_token
# is used. It will take whatever object is passed into the
# create_access_token method, and lets us define what custom claims
# should be added to the access token.
@jwt.user_claims_loader
def add_claims_to_access_token(user):
    return {'roles': user.roles}


# Create a function that will be called whenever create_access_token
# is used. It will take whatever object is passed into the
# create_access_token method, and lets us define what the identity
# of the access token should be.
@jwt.user_identity_loader
def user_identity_lookup(user):
    return user.username


@auth.route('/login', methods=['POST'])
def login():
    data = to_dict(request.form, request.args, request.get_json())
    username = data['username']
    password = data['password']

    if (username is None) or (password is None):
        return jsonify({"msg": "Bad username or password"}), 401

    log_user = User(username, password)

    if not log_user.IsUser:
        return jsonify({"msg": "Bad username or password"}), 401

    if len(log_user.dashboard_groups) == 0:
        return jsonify({"msg": "Forbidden"}), 403

    user = UserObject(log_user.username, log_user.roles)

    ret = {
        'access_token': create_access_token(identity=user),
        'refresh_token': create_refresh_token(identity=user)
    }
    return jsonify(ret), 200


# The jwt_refresh_token_required decorator insures a valid refresh
# token is present in the request before calling this endpoint. We
# can use the get_jwt_identity() function to get the identity of
# the refresh token, and use the create_access_token() function again
# to make a new access token for this identity.
@auth.route('/refresh', methods=['POST'])
@jwt_refresh_token_required
def refresh():
    user = UserObject(get_jwt_identity(), get_jwt_claims())
    ret = {
        'access_token': create_access_token(identity=user),
        'refresh_token': create_refresh_token(identity=user)
    }
    return jsonify(ret), 200


@auth.route('/protected', methods=['GET'])
@jwt_required
def protected():
    username = get_jwt_identity()
    roles = get_jwt_claims()
    print(roles)
    return jsonify(logged_in_as=username), 200

```

If I log in using my username and password, login function works just fine, I get my refresh token and access token. then If I hit `protected` endpoint, it works fine, it prints me all roles that I have store. 

However, if I hit `refresh` endpoint, then hit `protected` endpoint, I don't see any roles printed. I believe when I try to connect to `refresh` endpoint. I loose all role related data. when I print(get_jwt_claims()), it doesn't print anything in `refresh` end point. 

If I am not wrong, when I hist `refresh` endpoint then it is looking for roles base upon refresh token. which is null and that is creating issue.

issue_title: Upgrading from 0.3.4 to 0.3.5 broke "protocols"
issue_body:
Hello,

I have a demo application (basically a slightly modified version of the echo demo) that uses protocols, however after upgrading to 0.3.5 yesterday, I get an error in process_handshake_header() whenever I set matching protocols for server and client (Interestingly: omitting the protocols on either server or client, or giving server and client non-matching protocols works!)

``` python
  File "..\ws4py-0.3.5-py3.4.egg\ws4py\client\__init__.py", line 233, in connect
    self.protocols, self.extensions = self.process_handshake_header(headers)
  File "..\ws4py-0.3.5-py3.4.egg\ws4py\client\__init__.py", line 326, in process_handshake_header
    protocols = ','.join(value)
TypeError: sequence item 0: expected str instance, int found
```

Environment: Python 3.4 64 bit, cherrypy

``` python
    class EchoWebSocketHandler(WebSocket):
        # nothing special here, just echo the message

    cherrypy.quickstart(Root(args.host, args.port, args.ssl), '', config={
        '/ws_echo': {
            'tools.websocket.on': True,
            'tools.websocket.handler_cls': EchoWebSocketHandler,
            'tools.websocket.protocols': ['ws4py-echo', 'ws4py-test'], # omitting this line works
            },
        }
    )
```

``` python
            ws = EchoClient(ws_url, protocols=['ws4py-test', 'ws4py-echo']) # -> error
            #ws = EchoClient(ws_url, protocols=[]) # this works
            ws.connect()
```

Suggested fix in client/**init**.py:

``` python
            elif header == b'sec-websocket-protocol':
#                 protocols = ','.join(value) # original line
                protocols = [x.strip() for x in value.split(b',')] # replacement
```

I'm not even sure how the original code ever worked in the first place. Isn't value always a (byte)string? not sure how joining the (byte)string can result in an array.

If this really fixes the problem, you should consider to do the same for extensions.

Cheers, Mike

issue_title: Issue generating Array diff tree
issue_body:
(I did look at issue #30 , but that was after a successful creation of a patch. Apologies if I've misunderstood and it is the same issue)

I've been starting at this, and it looks like the issue lies in _split_by_common_seq

Take the following example
```python
import jsonpatch

src = [8, 7, 2, 1, 0, 9, 4, 3, 5, 6]
dest = [7, 2, 1, 0, 9, 4, 3, 6, 5, 8]

patch = jsonpatch.make_patch(src, dest)
```

When run, it produces the following error
```
$ python tmp.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "tmp.py", line 6, in <module>
    patch = jsonpatch.make_patch(src, dest)
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 171, in make_patch
    return JsonPatch.from_diff(src, dst)
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 322, in from_diff
    return cls(list(compare_values([], src, dst)))
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 297, in compare_values
    for operation in compare_lists(path, value, other):
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 730, in _optimize
    for item in operations:
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 649, in _compare
    for op, _ in _compare_with_shift(path, src, dst, left, right, 0):
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 672, in _compare_with_shift
    shift=shift):
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 672, in _compare_with_shift
    shift=shift):
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 675, in _compare_with_shift
    for item, shift in _compare_right(path, dst, right, shift):
  File "/space/TMP/crap/python-json-patch/jsonpatch.py", line 710, in _compare_right
    {'op': 'add', 'path': ptr.path, 'value': dst[idx]},
IndexError: list index out of range
```

If we look at the output of `_split_by_common_seq`
```
>>> jsonpatch._split_by_common_seq(src, dest)
[[(0, 1), None], [[(8, 9), None], [None, (9, 11)]]]
```
That (9, 11) is causing the issue, as dest is only 10 items long.


It looks like it's made this tree, which is valid.
```
[7, 2, 1, 0, 9, 4, 3]
   /   \
[8]     [6]
        / \
     [5]   [5, 8]
```
Based on that, the last tuple shuld be `(8, 10)`, which would work.

I've been staring at this and I can't work it out. I think it's something in the way the indexes are added together when recursing, but my brain can never handle logic to do with list indices.

Any suggestions would be great as it turns out this pattern of Array appears frequently in the data I'm dealing with.

    obj.__dict__[self.field.name] = self.field.to_python(value)
  File "/Users/hugo/Projects/src/github.com/yunojuno/python-semanticversion/semantic_version/django_fields.py", line 61, in to_python

  File "/Users/hugo/Projects/src/github.com/yunojuno/python-semanticversion/semantic_version/base.py", line 75, in __init__
    major, minor, patch, prerelease, build = self.parse(version_string, partial)
  File "/Users/hugo/Projects/src/github.com/yunojuno/python-semanticversion/semantic_version/base.py", line 192, in parse
    raise ValueError('Invalid version string: %r' % version_string)
ValueError: Invalid version string: u'None'
```

You can test this with the following test:

```python
def test_optional_field(self):
    """Test storing a null value in optional VersionField."""
    obj = models.PartialVersionModel()
    obj.save()
    self.assertIsNone(obj.optional)
    self.assertRaises(ValueError, models.PartialVersionModel.objects.get, id=obj.id)
```

This makes some sense, as the `VersionField.to_python` method doesn't check for 'None':

```
def to_python(self, value):
    """Converts any value to a base.Version field."""
    if value is None or value == '':
        return value
    if isinstance(value, base.Version):
        return value
    if self.coerce:
        return base.Version.coerce(value, partial=self.partial)
    else:
        return base.Version(value, partial=self.partial)
```

Possibly looking at the `BaseSemVerField.get_prep_value` method, which just str's the object value:

```python
def get_prep_value(self, obj):
    return str(obj)
```

This would turn `None` into `'None'`

issue_body:
Hi. I tried to use your serializer with the latest django rest framework. 

So I get such an error:
```

    from rest_framework.compat import OrderedDict
ImportError: cannot import name 'OrderedDict'
```

It seems that  `OrderedDict`  was removed from `from rest_framework.compat` because of python3. So I guess you need to add a `try ... catch` statement and try to import it from `collections` in case of python 3.


There is even a PR but it simply tries to take `OrderedDict` from the `collections`. Maybe that's not what you want.

issue_title: Memory leak using NI-SWITCH
issue_body: A few days ago while investigating https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/issues/268, I left the following code running for a few days (by accident, forgot to stop it): ``` import niswitch import time #with niswitch.Session('2532', False, True, '') as session: i = 0 while True: start = time.time() with niswitch.Session('', False, False, 'Simulate=1, DriverSetup=topology:2532/1-Wire 4x128 Matrix') as session: assert session.scan_mode == niswitch.ScanMode.BREAK_BEFORE_MAKE i = i + 1 end = time.time() print("Iteration {0}: {1:06.3f} seconds".format(i, (end-start), " seconds")) ``` A few days later, I'm pleasantly surprised that the program is still going strong after more than 375,000 iterations. But when I looked at Task Manager I was unpleasantly surprised to see that the python3.exe process was using 1,162,000 KB or RAM! Now, the program is creating and destroying a simulated DAQmx-based NI-SWITCH device. It's possible that the memory leak comes from the driver itself. If that's the case, we need to file an NI internal bug report. But it could also be that our Python code is somehow leaking memory in which case we need to fix it. Easiest way is to repeat the test without using simulation and/or using simulation on a CA4 device. If there's no leak then we can rule out Python bindings.
